facts.text: By 2050, about 86% of the developed world is predicted to be urbanized.
By 2050, about 64% of the developing world is predicted to be urbanized.
The predicted urban population growth is equivalent to approximately 3 billion new urbanites by 2050.
Most of the urban population growth expected by 2050 will occur in Africa and Asia.
In England and Wales, the proportion of the population living in cities with more than 20,000 people jumped from 17% in 1801 to 54% in 1891.
The earliest cities developed in the Indus Valley Civilization, Mesopotamia, and Egypt.
Until the 18th century, an equilibrium existed between rural and urban populations due to the relatively stagnant state of agriculture.
A significant increase in the percentage of the global urban population can be traced to the 1st millennium BCE.
The British Agricultural Revolution and Industrial Revolution in the late 18th century broke the long-standing rural-urban equilibrium.
Greater Seoul is home to 50% of South Korea's entire national population.
Greater Manila has a total population of 20 million, representing over 20% of the Philippines' national population.
Quezon City, with 2.7 million people, is the largest municipality in Greater Manila — not Manila itself.
Manila, the capital of the Philippines, has a population of only 1.6 million, smaller than Quezon City.
Greater Busan-Ulsan accounts for about 15% of South Korea's population with 8 million people.
Greater Osaka accounts for about 14% of Japan's population with 18 million people.
Urbanization refers to the proportion of national population living in urban areas, while urban growth refers to the absolute number of people in those areas.
The 'good west end and poor east end' division of large European cities likely developed because south-west winds carried coal smoke eastward.
Eric Hobsbawm described urban development in the early 19th century as 'a gigantic process of class segregation' in his 1962 book The Age of Revolution.
The Overseas Development Institute think tank has proposed policies encouraging labour-intensive industries to absorb less-skilled migrant workers.
Suburbanization in large developing countries can be seen as an attempt to balance the harmful aspects of urban life while retaining access to shared resources.
Dominant conurbations do not have to be single cities — Greater Manila is a conurbation rather than a single city.
Population in Greater Busan-Ulsan and Greater Osaka is moving toward the more dominant rivals Seoul and Tokyo respectively.
